question.text: Which of the following correctly matches the sections of the Negotiable Instruments Act 1881 with the instruments they define?

answer.text: Section 4 — Bill of Exchange; Section 5 — Promissory Note; Section 6 — Cheque
Section 4 — Cheque; Section 5 — Promissory Note; Section 6 — Bill of Exchange
Section 4 — Promissory Note; Section 5 — Cheque; Section 6 — Bill of Exchange
Section 4 — Promissory Note; Section 5 — Bill of Exchange; Section 6 — Cheque

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Under the Negotiable Instruments Act 1881: Section 4 defines a Promissory Note, Section 5 defines a Bill of Exchange, and Section 6 defines a Cheque. Section 13 defines the term "negotiable instrument" itself. **The Trap:** The first option swaps Promissory Note and Bill of Exchange, which is the most common confusion. Candidates who remember only one or two sections correctly may fall for partially correct options. **Cross-Exam Context:** These section-wise definitions are directly tested in NISM Series X-A exam and are also relevant for banking certifications. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Remember the sequence P-B-C (4-5-6): Promissory note, Bill of exchange, Cheque. A promissory note is a promise to pay; a bill of exchange is an order to pay; a cheque is a special bill of exchange drawn on a banker.
